title: Finding derivative with fundamental theorem of calculus: chain rule
description: Use the chain rule and the fundamental theorem of calculus to find the derivative of definite integrals with lower or upper limits other than x.
topic_path: The fundamental theorem of calculus and accumulation functions
Finding derivative with fundamental theorem of calculus: chain rule

hints: The following hints will explain *how to* solve this problem, but it's really important that we understand *why* this works. To get that understanding, we highly recommend that you [watch Sal solve a similar problem](/math/integral-calculus/indefinite-definite-integrals/fundamental-theorem-of-calculus/v/applying-the-fundamental-theorem-of-calculus).

Here are the steps to solve this problem:

$\phantom{=}F'(x)$ 

$\displaystyle = \dfrac{d}{dx} \int_{-5}^{3x}\sqrt{15-t}\,dt\,$

The following hints will explain *how to* solve this problem, but it's really important that we understand *why* this works. To get that understanding, we highly recommend that you [watch Sal solve a similar problem](/math/integral-calculus/indefinite-definite-integrals/fundamental-theorem-of-calculus/v/applying-the-fundamental-theorem-of-calculus).

Here are the steps to solve this problem:

$\phantom{=}F'(x)$ 

$\displaystyle = \dfrac{d}{dx} \int_{2}^{2x}\sqrt{15-t}\,dt\,$

The following hints will explain *how to* solve this problem, but it's really important that we understand *why* this works. To get that understanding, we highly recommend that you [watch Sal solve a similar problem](/math/integral-calculus/indefinite-definite-integrals/fundamental-theorem-of-calculus/v/applying-the-fundamental-theorem-of-calculus).

Here are the steps to solve this problem:

$\phantom{=}F'(x)$ 

$\displaystyle = \dfrac{d}{dx} \int_{1}^{2x}\sqrt{1 + t^3}\,dt\,$

The following hints will explain *how to* solve this problem, but it's really important that we understand *why* this works. To get that understanding, we highly recommend that you [watch Sal solve a similar problem](/math/integral-calculus/indefinite-definite-integrals/fundamental-theorem-of-calculus/v/applying-the-fundamental-theorem-of-calculus).

Here are the steps to solve this problem:

$\phantom{=}F'(x)$ 

$\displaystyle = \dfrac{d}{dx} \int_{1}^{2x}\dfrac{t^2}{t^2 + 1} \, dt$

The following hints will explain *how to* solve this problem, but it's really important that we understand *why* this works. To get that understanding, we highly recommend that you [watch Sal solve a similar problem](/math/integral-calculus/indefinite-definite-integrals/fundamental-theorem-of-calculus/v/applying-the-fundamental-theorem-of-calculus).

Here are the steps to solve this problem:

$\phantom{=}F'(x)$ 

$\displaystyle = \dfrac{d}{dx} \int_{-2}^{2x} (3t^2 + 2t)  \, dt$
